flashcards.back: A memory management technique that creates an illusion of having a large contiguous address space by using both RAM and disk space. It allows programs larger than physical memory to execute.
A paging technique where pages are loaded into memory only when they are referenced (demand). Pages not initially loaded stay on disk until a page fault occurs.
An interrupt that occurs when a program accesses a page not currently in physical memory. The OS must then bring the required page from secondary storage into RAM.
Excessive paging activity where the system spends more time swapping pages in and out of memory than executing processes. It occurs when processes demand more frames than available.

flashcards.front: What is the Working Set of a process?
flashcards.back: The set of pages that a process has referenced during the most recent time interval (window). Keeping the working set in memory minimizes page faults.
The tendency of a processor to access the same memory locations repeatedly over a short time period. This principle makes virtual memory efficient.

flashcards.front: What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?
flashcards.back: A hardware cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations to speed up memory access. It is a fully associative cache.
A memory optimization technique where fork() creates shared pages initially. Only when a process writes to a page does the OS create a separate copy for that process.

flashcards.front: What is an Inverted Page Table?
flashcards.back: A page table structure that stores one entry per physical frame instead of per virtual page. It reduces memory overhead but increases lookup time.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Global and Local page replacement?
flashcards.back: Global replacement allows any process to replace any frame. Local replacement restricts each process to replace only from its own allocated set of frames.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Valid-Invalid bit in a page table?
flashcards.back: It indicates whether the referenced page is currently in physical memory (valid) or not (invalid). If invalid, a page fault occurs upon access.

flashcards.front: What is a page replacement algorithm?
flashcards.back: An algorithm that decides which page to remove from memory when a new page needs to be loaded and no free frames are available. Examples: FIFO, LRU, Optimal.

flashcards.front: What is the principle of Optimal Page Replacement?
flashcards.back: The optimal algorithm replaces the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future. It provides the minimum possible page faults but is not implementable in practice.
Belady's Anomaly is the phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames can cause an increase in page faults. It occurs with the FIFO algorithm.
